items.correct: No — rituals carry symbolic meaning, transmit belief, mark transitions and, as Durkheim argued, generate social cohesion and shared identity within the community.
items.misconception: Rituals are empty habits performed without purpose or function.
items.random_wrong: Rituals may only be performed by ordained religious leaders.
items.why: Students dismiss repetition as meaningless, missing the analytical point that repetition itself encodes meaning and sustains community across generations.

items.q: What are the three stages of a rite of passage according to van Gennep?
items.correct: Separation from the previous status, a transitional liminal stage, and incorporation into the new status.
items.misconception: The three stages are birth, marriage and death.
items.random_wrong: The three stages are dawn, noon and dusk ceremonies.
items.why: Students name occasions for rites of passage instead of the structural stages every such rite passes through — a key distinction in ritual theory.

items.q: Is pilgrimage a compulsory duty in every religion?
items.correct: No — obligation varies: Hajj is obligatory once for Muslims who are able, while most Christian and Hindu pilgrimages are voluntary acts of devotion.
items.misconception: All religions require pilgrimage as a compulsory duty like the Hajj.
items.random_wrong: Pilgrimage means permanently relocating to a holy city.
items.why: Students generalise the Islamic obligation to other faiths; comparing the status of practices across traditions is central to this course.

items.q: What are van Gennep's three stages of a rite of passage?
items.why: Correct answer: Separation, transition, incorporation. “Birth, marriage, death” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which scholar linked the liminal phase of ritual with 'communitas'?
items.why: Correct answer: Victor Turner. “Emile Durkheim” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The Hajj is a pilgrimage to which city?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Mecca. “Jerusalem” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is a rite of passage?
items.correct: A ritual marking a person's transition between life stages
items.random_wrong: A text read aloud during worship
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A ritual marking a person's transition between life stages. “A daily prayer routine” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many stages did van Gennep identify in rites of passage?
items.why: The real figure is 3 stages. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
